revision.text:
{{Short description|Ancient Japanese history text}}{{italic title}} {{Shintoism}} The {{nihongo|'''''Shoku Nihongi'''''|続日本紀|}} is an imperially-commissioned [[Japanese history]] text. Completed in 797, it is the second of the ''[[Rikkokushi|Six National Histories]]'', coming directly after the {{lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} and followed by ''[[Nihon Kōki]]''. [[Fujiwara no Tsugutada]] and [[Sugano no Mamichi]] served as the primary editors. It is one of the most important primary historical sources for information about Japan's [[Nara period]].
The work covers the 95-year period from the beginning of [[Emperor Monmu]]'s reign in 697 until the 10th year of [[Emperor Kanmu]]'s reign in 791, spanning nine imperial reigns. It was completed in 797 AD.<ref>{{citation |url= http://journal.oraltradition.org/issues/24i/bender |title=Performative Loci of ''Shoku Nihongi'' Edicts, 749–770 |last= Bender|first= Ross|date= March 2009 |work= Journal of Oral Tradition|volume=24|issue=1|pages=249–268}}</ref>
The text is forty volumes in length. It is primarily written in [[kanbun]], a Japanese form of [[Classical Chinese]], as was normal for formal Japanese texts at the time.<ref>''Rikkokushi'' (『六国史』), by [[Tarō Sakamoto]] (坂本太郎) (Yoshikawa Kobunkan (吉川弘文館), 1970, republished 1994)</ref> However, a number of "senmyō" 宣命 or "imperial edicts" contained within the text are written in a script known as "senmyō-gaki", which preserves particles and verb endings phonographically.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1245 |title=Senmyō |access-date=2012-02-27 |last=Motosawa |first=Masafumi |date=2007-03-28 |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto }}</ref>
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== * {{NKBJ}} * {{NKBD}} *[http://www.j-texts.com/sheet/shoku.html Text of the ''Shoku Nihongi''] (Japanese) *[https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Shoku%20Nihongi.html Further information and text of the ''Shoku Nihongi''] at [[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] *[http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_02450/ri05_02450_0016/ manuscript scans at Waseda University Library]
{{Rikkokushi}}
[[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Shoku Nihongi]]

{{Short description|Ancient Japanese history text}}{{italic title}} {{Shintoism}} The {{nihongo|'''''Shoku Nihongi'''''|続日本紀|}} is an imperially-commissioned [[Japanese history]] text. Completed in 797, it is the second of the ''[[Rikkokushi|Six National Histories]]'', coming directly after the {{lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} and followed by ''[[Nihon Kōki]]''. [[Fujiwara no Tsugutada]] and [[Sugano no Mamichi]] served as the primary editors. It is one of the most important primary historical sources for information about Japan's [[Nara period]].
The work covers the 95-year period from the beginning of [[Emperor Monmu]]'s reign in 697 until the 10th year of [[Emperor Kanmu]]'s reign in 791, spanning nine imperial reigns. It was completed in 797 AD.<ref>{{citation |url= http://journal.oraltradition.org/issues/24i/bender |title=Performative Loci of ''Shoku Nihongi'' Edicts, 749–770 |last= Bender|first= Ross|date= March 2009 |work= Journal of Oral Tradition|volume=24|issue=1|pages=249–268}}</ref>
The text is forty volumes in length. It is primarily written in [[kanbun]], a Japanese form of [[Classical Chinese]], as was normal for formal Japanese texts at the time.<ref>''Rikkokushi'' (『六国史』), by [[Tarō Sakamoto]] (坂本太郎) (Yoshikawa Kobunkan (吉川弘文館), 1970, republished 1994)</ref> However, a number of "senmyō" 宣命 or "imperial edicts" contained within the text are written in a script known as "senmyō-gaki", which preserves particles and verb endings phonographically.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1245 |title=Senmyō |access-date=2012-02-27 |last=Motosawa |first=Masafumi |date=2007-03-28 |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto }}</ref>

{{Short description|Ancient Japanese history text}}{{italic title}} {{Shintoism}} The {{nihongo|'''''Shoku Nihongi'''''|続日本紀|}} is an imperially-commissioned [[Japanese history]] text. Completed in 797, it is the second of the ''[[Rikkokushi|Six National Histories]]'', coming directly after the {{lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} and followed by ''[[Nihon Kōki]]''. [[Fujiwara no Tsugutada]] and [[Sugano no Mamichi]] served as the primary editors. It is one of the most important primary historical sources for information about Japan's [[Nara period]].
The work covers the 95-year period from the beginning of [[Emperor Monmu]]'s reign in 697 until the 10th year of [[Emperor Kanmu]]'s reign in 791, spanning nine imperial reigns. It was completed in 797 AD.<ref>{{citation |url= http://journal.oraltradition.org/issues/24i/bender |title=Performative Loci of ''Shoku Nihongi'' Edicts, 749–770 |last= Bender|first= Ross|date= March 2009 |work= Journal of Oral Tradition|volume=24|issue=1|pages=249–268}}</ref>
The text is forty volumes in length. It is primarily written in [[kanbun]], a Japanese form of [[Classical Chinese]], as was normal for formal Japanese texts at the time.<ref>''Rikkokushi'' (『六国史』), by [[Tarō Sakamoto]] (坂本太郎) (Yoshikawa Kobunkan (吉川弘文館), 1970, republished 1994)</ref> However, a number of "senmyō" 宣命 or "imperial edicts" contained within the text are written in a script known as "senmyō-gaki", which preserves particles and verb endings phonographically.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1245 |title=Senmyō |access-date=2012-02-27 |last=Motosawa |first=Masafumi |date=2007-03-28 |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto }}</ref>

It is primarily written in [[kanbun]], a Japanese form of [[Classical Chinese]], as was normal for formal Japanese texts at the time.<ref>''Rikkokushi'' (『六国史』), by [[Tarō Sakamoto]] (坂本太郎) (Yoshikawa Kobunkan (吉川弘文館), 1970, republished 1994)</ref> However, a number of "senmyō" 宣命 or "imperial edicts" contained within the text are written in a script known as "senmyō-gaki", which preserves particles and verb endings phonographically.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1245 |title=Senmyō |access-date=2012-02-27 |last=Motosawa |first=Masafumi |date=2007-03-28 |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto }}</ref>

Completed in 797, it is the second of the ''[[Rikkokushi|Six National Histories]]'', coming directly after the {{lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} and followed by ''[[:en:Nihon Kōki|Nihon Kōki]]''. [[:en:Fujiwara no Tsugutada|Fujiwara no Tsugutada]] and [[:en:Sugano no Mamichi|Sugano no Mamichi]] served as the primary editors. It is one of the most important primary historical sources for information about Japan's [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]].
The work covers the 95-year period from the beginning of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]]'s reign in 697 until the 10th year of [[Emperor Kanmu]]'s reign in 791, spanning nine imperial reigns. It was completed in 797 AD.<ref>{{citation |url= http://journal.oraltradition.org/issues/24i/bender |title=Performative Loci of ''Shoku Nihongi'' Edicts, 749–770 |last= Bender|first= Ross|date= March 2009 |work= Journal of Oral Tradition|volume=24|issue=1|pages=249–268}}</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شوكو نيهونغي]][[ca:Shoku Nihongi]][[cs:Šoku Nihongi]][[de:Shoku Nihongi]][[en:Shoku Nihongi]][[es:Shoku Nihongi]][[eu:Shoku Nihongi]][[fa:شوکو نیهونگی]][[ff:Shoku Nihongi]][[fr:Shoku Nihongi]][[id:Shoku Nihongi]][[it:Shoku Nihongi]][[ja:続日本紀]][[ko:속일본기]][[ms:Shoku Nihongi]][[pt:Shoku Nihongi]][[ru:Сёку нихонги]][[simple:Shoku Nihongi]][[sl:Šoku Nihongi]][[th:โชกุนิฮงงิ]][[tr:Shoku Nihongi]][[uk:Сьоку Ніхонґі]][[zh:續日本紀]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q611399]] Wikidata name: Shoku Nihongi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Ancient Japanese history text}}{{italic title}} {{Shintoism}} The {{nihongo|'''''Shoku Nihongi'''''|続日本紀|}} is an imperially-commissioned [[:en:Japanese history|Japanese history]] text. Completed in 797, it is the second of the ''[[Rikkokushi|Six National Histories]]'', coming directly after the {{lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} and followed by ''[[:en:Nihon Kōki|Nihon Kōki]]''. [[:en:Fujiwara no Tsugutada|Fujiwara no Tsugutada]] and [[:en:Sugano no Mamichi|Sugano no Mamichi]] served as the primary editors. It is one of the most important primary historical sources for information about Japan's [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]].
The work covers the 95-year period from the beginning of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]]'s reign in 697 until the 10th year of [[Emperor Kanmu]]'s reign in 791, spanning nine imperial reigns. It was completed in 797 AD.<ref>{{citation |url= http://journal.oraltradition.org/issues/24i/bender |title=Performative Loci of ''Shoku Nihongi'' Edicts, 749–770 |last= Bender|first= Ross|date= March 2009 |work= Journal of Oral Tradition|volume=24|issue=1|pages=249–268}}</ref>
The text is forty volumes in length. It is primarily written in [[:en:Kanbun|Kanbun]], a Japanese form of [[:en:Classical Chinese|Classical Chinese]], as was normal for formal Japanese texts at the time.<ref>''Rikkokushi'' (『六国史』), by [[Tarō Sakamoto]] (坂本太郎) (Yoshikawa Kobunkan (吉川弘文館), 1970, republished 1994)</ref> However, a number of "senmyō" 宣命 or "imperial edicts" contained within the text are written in a script known as "senmyō-gaki", which preserves particles and verb endings phonographically.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1245 |title=Senmyō |access-date=2012-02-27 |last=Motosawa |first=Masafumi |date=2007-03-28 |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto }}</ref>
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== * {{NKBJ}} * {{NKBD}} *[http://www.j-texts.com/sheet/shoku.html Text of the ''Shoku Nihongi''] (Japanese) *[https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Shoku%20Nihongi.html Further information and text of the ''Shoku Nihongi''] at [[:en:Japanese Historical Text Initiative|Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] *[http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_02450/ri05_02450_0016/ manuscript scans at Waseda University Library]
{{Rikkokushi}} {{japan-hist-book-stub}}

}}

Completed in 797, it is the second of the ''[[Rikkokushi|Six National Histories]]'', coming directly after the {{lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} and followed by ''[[:en:Nihon Kōki|Nihon Kōki]]''. [[:en:Fujiwara no Tsugutada|Fujiwara no Tsugutada]] and [[:en:Sugano no Mamichi|Sugano no Mamichi]] served as the primary editors. It is one of the most important primary historical sources for information about Japan's [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]].
The work covers the 95-year period from the beginning of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]]'s reign in 697 until the 10th year of [[Emperor Kanmu]]'s reign in 791, spanning nine imperial reigns. It was completed in 797 AD.<ref>{{citation |url= http://journal.oraltradition.org/issues/24i/bender |title=Performative Loci of ''Shoku Nihongi'' Edicts, 749–770 |last= Bender|first= Ross|date= March 2009 |work= Journal of Oral Tradition|volume=24|issue=1|pages=249–268}}</ref>
The text is forty volumes in length. It is primarily written in [[:en:Kanbun|Kanbun]], a Japanese form of [[:en:Classical Chinese|Classical Chinese]], as was normal for formal Japanese texts at the time.<ref>''Rikkokushi'' (『六国史』), by [[Tarō Sakamoto]] (坂本太郎) (Yoshikawa Kobunkan (吉川弘文館), 1970, republished 1994)</ref> However, a number of "senmyō" 宣命 or "imperial edicts" contained within the text are written in a script known as "senmyō-gaki", which preserves particles and verb endings phonographically.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1245 |title=Senmyō |access-date=2012-02-27 |last=Motosawa |first=Masafumi |date=2007-03-28 |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto }}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شوكو نيهونغي]][[ca:Shoku Nihongi]][[cs:Šoku Nihongi]][[de:Shoku Nihongi]][[en:Shoku Nihongi]][[es:Shoku Nihongi]][[eu:Shoku Nihongi]][[fa:شوکو نیهونگی]][[ff:Shoku Nihongi]][[fr:Shoku Nihongi]][[id:Shoku Nihongi]][[it:Shoku Nihongi]][[ja:続日本紀]][[ko:속일본기]][[ms:Shoku Nihongi]][[pt:Shoku Nihongi]][[ru:Сёку нихонги]][[simple:Shoku Nihongi]][[sl:Šoku Nihongi]][[th:โชกุนิฮงงิ]][[tr:Shoku Nihongi]][[uk:Сьоку Ніхонґі]][[zh:續日本紀]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q611399]] Wikidata name: Shoku Nihongi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Ancient Japanese history text}}{{italic title}} {{Shintoism}} The {{nihongo|'''''Shoku Nihongi'''''|続日本紀|}} is an imperially-commissioned [[:en:Japanese history|Japanese history]] text. Completed in 797, it is the second of the ''[[Rikkokushi|Six National Histories]]'', coming directly after the {{lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} and followed by ''[[:en:Nihon Kōki|Nihon Kōki]]''. [[:en:Fujiwara no Tsugutada|Fujiwara no Tsugutada]] and [[:en:Sugano no Mamichi|Sugano no Mamichi]] served as the primary editors. It is one of the most important primary historical sources for information about Japan's [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]].
The work covers the 95-year period from the beginning of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]]'s reign in 697 until the 10th year of [[Emperor Kanmu]]'s reign in 791, spanning nine imperial reigns. It was completed in 797 AD.<ref>{{citation |url= http://journal.oraltradition.org/issues/24i/bender |title=Performative Loci of ''Shoku Nihongi'' Edicts, 749–770 |last= Bender|first= Ross|date= March 2009 |work= Journal of Oral Tradition|volume=24|issue=1|pages=249–268}}</ref>
The text is forty volumes in length. It is primarily written in [[:en:Kanbun|Kanbun]], a Japanese form of [[:en:Classical Chinese|Classical Chinese]], as was normal for formal Japanese texts at the time.<ref>''Rikkokushi'' (『六国史』), by [[Tarō Sakamoto]] (坂本太郎) (Yoshikawa Kobunkan (吉川弘文館), 1970, republished 1994)</ref> However, a number of "senmyō" 宣命 or "imperial edicts" contained within the text are written in a script known as "senmyō-gaki", which preserves particles and verb endings phonographically.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1245 |title=Senmyō |access-date=2012-02-27 |last=Motosawa |first=Masafumi |date=2007-03-28 |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto }}</ref>

It was completed in 797 AD.<ref>{{citation |url= http://journal.oraltradition.org/issues/24i/bender |title=Performative Loci of ''Shoku Nihongi'' Edicts, 749–770 |last= Bender|first= Ross|date= March 2009 |work= Journal of Oral Tradition|volume=24|issue=1|pages=249–268}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شوكو نيهونغي]][[ca:Shoku Nihongi]][[cs:Šoku Nihongi]][[de:Shoku Nihongi]][[en:Shoku Nihongi]][[es:Shoku Nihongi]][[eu:Shoku Nihongi]][[fa:شوکو نیهونگی]][[ff:Shoku Nihongi]][[fr:Shoku Nihongi]][[id:Shoku Nihongi]][[it:Shoku Nihongi]][[ja:続日本紀]][[ko:속일본기]][[ms:Shoku Nihongi]][[pt:Shoku Nihongi]][[ru:Сёку нихонги]][[simple:Shoku Nihongi]][[sl:Šoku Nihongi]][[th:โชกุนิฮงงิ]][[tr:Shoku Nihongi]][[uk:Сьоку Ніхонґі]][[zh:續日本紀]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q611399]] Wikidata name: Shoku Nihongi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Ancient Japanese history text}}{{italic title}} {{Shintoism}} The {{nihongo|'''''Shoku Nihongi'''''|続日本紀|}} is an imperially-commissioned [[:en:Japanese history|Japanese history]] text. Completed in 797, it is the second of the ''[[Rikkokushi|Six National Histories]]'', coming directly after the {{lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} and followed by ''[[:en:Nihon Kōki|Nihon Kōki]]''. [[:en:Fujiwara no Tsugutada|Fujiwara no Tsugutada]] and [[:en:Sugano no Mamichi|Sugano no Mamichi]] served as the primary editors. It is one of the most important primary historical sources for information about Japan's [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]].
The work covers the 95-year period from the beginning of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]]'s reign in 697 until the 10th year of [[Emperor Kanmu]]'s reign in 791, spanning nine imperial reigns. It was completed in 797 AD.<ref>{{citation |url= http://journal.oraltradition.org/issues/24i/bender |title=Performative Loci of ''Shoku Nihongi'' Edicts, 749–770 |last= Bender|first= Ross|date= March 2009 |work= Journal of Oral Tradition|volume=24|issue=1|pages=249–268}}</ref>
The text is forty volumes in length. It is primarily written in [[:en:Kanbun|Kanbun]], a Japanese form of [[:en:Classical Chinese|Classical Chinese]], as was normal for formal Japanese texts at the time.<ref>''Rikkokushi'' (『六国史』), by [[Tarō Sakamoto]] (坂本太郎) (Yoshikawa Kobunkan (吉川弘文館), 1970, republished 1994)</ref> However, a number of "senmyō" 宣命 or "imperial edicts" contained within the text are written in a script known as "senmyō-gaki", which preserves particles and verb endings phonographically.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1245 |title=Senmyō |access-date=2012-02-27 |last=Motosawa |first=Masafumi |date=2007-03-28 |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto }}</ref>

Completed in 797, it is the second of the ''[[Rikkokushi|Six National Histories]]'', coming directly after the {{lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} and followed by ''[[:en:Nihon Kōki|Nihon Kōki]]''. [[:en:Fujiwara no Tsugutada|Fujiwara no Tsugutada]] and [[:en:Sugano no Mamichi|Sugano no Mamichi]] served as the primary editors. It is one of the most important primary historical sources for information about Japan's [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]].
The work covers the 95-year period from the beginning of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]]'s reign in 697 until the 10th year of [[Emperor Kanmu]]'s reign in 791, spanning nine imperial reigns. It was completed in 797 AD.<ref>{{citation |url= http://journal.oraltradition.org/issues/24i/bender |title=Performative Loci of ''Shoku Nihongi'' Edicts, 749–770 |last= Bender|first= Ross|date= March 2009 |work= Journal of Oral Tradition|volume=24|issue=1|pages=249–268}}</ref>
The text is forty volumes in length. It is primarily written in [[:en:Kanbun|Kanbun]], a Japanese form of [[:en:Classical Chinese|Classical Chinese]], as was normal for formal Japanese texts at the time.<ref>''Rikkokushi'' (『六国史』), by [[Tarō Sakamoto]] (坂本太郎) (Yoshikawa Kobunkan (吉川弘文館), 1970, republished 1994)</ref> However, a number of "senmyō" 宣命 or "imperial edicts" contained within the text are written in a script known as "senmyō-gaki", which preserves particles and verb endings phonographically.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1245 |title=Senmyō |access-date=2012-02-27 |last=Motosawa |first=Masafumi |date=2007-03-28 |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto }}</ref>

Completed in 797, it is the second of the ''[[Rikkokushi|Six National Histories]]'', coming directly after the {{lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} and followed by ''[[:en:Nihon Kōki|Nihon Kōki]]''. [[:en:Fujiwara no Tsugutada|Fujiwara no Tsugutada]] and [[:en:Sugano no Mamichi|Sugano no Mamichi]] served as the primary editors. It is one of the most important primary historical sources for information about Japan's [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]].
The work covers the 95-year period from the beginning of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]]'s reign in 697 until the 10th year of [[Emperor Kanmu]]'s reign in 791, spanning nine imperial reigns. It was completed in 797 AD.<ref>{{citation |url= http://journal.oraltradition.org/issues/24i/bender |title=Performative Loci of ''Shoku Nihongi'' Edicts, 749–770 |last= Bender|first= Ross|date= March 2009 |work= Journal of Oral Tradition|volume=24|issue=1|pages=249–268}}</ref>
The text is forty volumes in length. It is primarily written in [[:en:Kanbun|Kanbun]], a Japanese form of [[:en:Classical Chinese|Classical Chinese]], as was normal for formal Japanese texts at the time.<ref>''Rikkokushi'' (『六国史』), by [[Tarō Sakamoto]] (坂本太郎) (Yoshikawa Kobunkan (吉川弘文館), 1970, republished 1994)</ref> However, a number of "senmyō" 宣命 or "imperial edicts" contained within the text are written in a script known as "senmyō-gaki", which preserves particles and verb endings phonographically.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1245 |title=Senmyō |access-date=2012-02-27 |last=Motosawa |first=Masafumi |date=2007-03-28 |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto }}</ref>

Completed in 797, it is the second of the ''[[Rikkokushi|Six National Histories]]'', coming directly after the {{lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} and followed by ''[[:en:Nihon Kōki|Nihon Kōki]]''. [[:en:Fujiwara no Tsugutada|Fujiwara no Tsugutada]] and [[:en:Sugano no Mamichi|Sugano no Mamichi]] served as the primary editors. It is one of the most important primary historical sources for information about Japan's [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]].
The work covers the 95-year period from the beginning of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]]'s reign in 697 until the 10th year of [[Emperor Kanmu]]'s reign in 791, spanning nine imperial reigns. It was completed in 797 AD.<ref>{{citation |url= http://journal.oraltradition.org/issues/24i/bender |title=Performative Loci of ''Shoku Nihongi'' Edicts, 749–770 |last= Bender|first= Ross|date= March 2009 |work= Journal of Oral Tradition|volume=24|issue=1|pages=249–268}}</ref>
The text is forty volumes in length. It is primarily written in [[:en:Kanbun|Kanbun]], a Japanese form of [[:en:Classical Chinese|Classical Chinese]], as was normal for formal Japanese texts at the time.<ref>''Rikkokushi'' (『六国史』), by [[Tarō Sakamoto]] (坂本太郎) (Yoshikawa Kobunkan (吉川弘文館), 1970, republished 1994)</ref> However, a number of "senmyō" 宣命 or "imperial edicts" contained within the text are written in a script known as "senmyō-gaki", which preserves particles and verb endings phonographically.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1245 |title=Senmyō |access-date=2012-02-27 |last=Motosawa |first=Masafumi |date=2007-03-28 |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto }}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شوكو نيهونغي]][[ca:Shoku Nihongi]][[cs:Šoku Nihongi]][[de:Shoku Nihongi]][[en:Shoku Nihongi]][[es:Shoku Nihongi]][[eu:Shoku Nihongi]][[fa:شوکو نیهونگی]][[ff:Shoku Nihongi]][[fr:Shoku Nihongi]][[id:Shoku Nihongi]][[it:Shoku Nihongi]][[ja:続日本紀]][[ko:속일본기]][[ms:Shoku Nihongi]][[pt:Shoku Nihongi]][[ru:Сёку нихонги]][[simple:Shoku Nihongi]][[sl:Šoku Nihongi]][[th:โชกุนิฮงงิ]][[tr:Shoku Nihongi]][[uk:Сьоку Ніхонґі]][[zh:續日本紀]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شوكو نيهونغي]][[ca:Shoku Nihongi]][[cs:Šoku Nihongi]][[de:Shoku Nihongi]][[en:Shoku Nihongi]][[es:Shoku Nihongi]][[eu:Shoku Nihongi]][[fa:شوکو نیهونگی]][[ff:Shoku Nihongi]][[fr:Shoku Nihongi]][[id:Shoku Nihongi]][[it:Shoku Nihongi]][[ja:続日本紀]][[ko:속일본기]][[ms:Shoku Nihongi]][[pt:Shoku Nihongi]][[ru:Сёку нихонги]][[simple:Shoku Nihongi]][[sl:Šoku Nihongi]][[th:โชกุนิฮงงิ]][[tr:Shoku Nihongi]][[uk:Сьоку Ніхонґі]][[zh:續日本紀]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شوكو نيهونغي]][[ca:Shoku Nihongi]][[cs:Šoku Nihongi]][[de:Shoku Nihongi]][[en:Shoku Nihongi]][[es:Shoku Nihongi]][[eu:Shoku Nihongi]][[fa:شوکو نیهونگی]][[ff:Shoku Nihongi]][[fr:Shoku Nihongi]][[id:Shoku Nihongi]][[it:Shoku Nihongi]][[ja:続日本紀]][[ko:속일본기]][[ms:Shoku Nihongi]][[pt:Shoku Nihongi]][[ru:Сёку нихонги]][[simple:Shoku Nihongi]][[sl:Šoku Nihongi]][[th:โชกุนิฮงงิ]][[tr:Shoku Nihongi]][[uk:Сьоку Ніхонґі]][[zh:續日本紀]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q611399]] Wikidata name: Shoku Nihongi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Ancient Japanese history text}}{{italic title}} {{Shintoism}} The {{nihongo|'''''Shoku Nihongi'''''|続日本紀|}} is an imperially-commissioned [[:en:Japanese history|Japanese history]] text. Completed in 797, it is the second of the ''[[Rikkokushi|Six National Histories]]'', coming directly after the {{lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} and followed by ''[[:en:Nihon Kōki|Nihon Kōki]]''. [[:en:Fujiwara no Tsugutada|Fujiwara no Tsugutada]] and [[:en:Sugano no Mamichi|Sugano no Mamichi]] served as the primary editors. It is one of the most important primary historical sources for information about Japan's [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]].
The work covers the 95-year period from the beginning of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]]'s reign in 697 until the 10th year of [[Emperor Kanmu]]'s reign in 791, spanning nine imperial reigns. It was completed in 797 AD.<ref>{{citation |url= http://journal.oraltradition.org/issues/24i/bender |title=Performative Loci of ''Shoku Nihongi'' Edicts, 749–770 |last= Bender|first= Ross|date= March 2009 |work= Journal of Oral Tradition|volume=24|issue=1|pages=249–268}}</ref>
The text is forty volumes in length. It is primarily written in [[:en:Kanbun|Kanbun]], a Japanese form of [[:en:Classical Chinese|Classical Chinese]], as was normal for formal Japanese texts at the time.<ref>''Rikkokushi'' (『六国史』), by [[Tarō Sakamoto]] (坂本太郎) (Yoshikawa Kobunkan (吉川弘文館), 1970, republished 1994)</ref> However, a number of "senmyō" 宣命 or "imperial edicts" contained within the text are written in a script known as "senmyō-gaki", which preserves particles and verb endings phonographically.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1245 |title=Senmyō |access-date=2012-02-27 |last=Motosawa |first=Masafumi |date=2007-03-28 |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto }}</ref>
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== * {{NKBJ}} * {{NKBD}} *[http://www.j-texts.com/sheet/shoku.html Text of the ''Shoku Nihongi''] (Japanese) *[https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Shoku%20Nihongi.html Further information and text of the ''Shoku Nihongi''] at [[:en:Japanese Historical Text Initiative|Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] *[http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_02450/ri05_02450_0016/ manuscript scans at Waseda University Library]

Completed in 797, it is the second of the ''[[Rikkokushi|Six National Histories]]'', coming directly after the {{lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} and followed by ''[[:en:Nihon Kōki|Nihon Kōki]]''. [[:en:Fujiwara no Tsugutada|Fujiwara no Tsugutada]] and [[:en:Sugano no Mamichi|Sugano no Mamichi]] served as the primary editors. It is one of the most important primary historical sources for information about Japan's [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]].
The work covers the 95-year period from the beginning of [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]]'s reign in 697 until the 10th year of [[Emperor Kanmu]]'s reign in 791, spanning nine imperial reigns. It was completed in 797 AD.<ref>{{citation |url= http://journal.oraltradition.org/issues/24i/bender |title=Performative Loci of ''Shoku Nihongi'' Edicts, 749–770 |last= Bender|first= Ross|date= March 2009 |work= Journal of Oral Tradition|volume=24|issue=1|pages=249–268}}</ref>
The text is forty volumes in length. It is primarily written in [[:en:Kanbun|Kanbun]], a Japanese form of [[:en:Classical Chinese|Classical Chinese]], as was normal for formal Japanese texts at the time.<ref>''Rikkokushi'' (『六国史』), by [[Tarō Sakamoto]] (坂本太郎) (Yoshikawa Kobunkan (吉川弘文館), 1970, republished 1994)</ref> However, a number of "senmyō" 宣命 or "imperial edicts" contained within the text are written in a script known as "senmyō-gaki", which preserves particles and verb endings phonographically.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1245 |title=Senmyō |access-date=2012-02-27 |last=Motosawa |first=Masafumi |date=2007-03-28 |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto }}</ref>

Completed in 797, it is the second of the ''[[Rikkokushi|Six National Histories]]'', coming directly after the {{lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} and followed by ''{{ill|Nihon Kōki|en|Nihon Kōki|WD=Q1077029|lt=Nihon Kōki}}''. [[:en:Fujiwara no Tsugutada|Fujiwara no Tsugutada]] and {{ill|Sugano no Mamichi|en|Sugano no Mamichi|WD=Q11619464|lt=Sugano no Mamichi}} served as the primary editors. It is one of the most important primary historical sources for information about Japan's {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}.
The work covers the 95-year period from the beginning of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en|Emperor Monmu|WD=Q357246|lt=Emperor Monmu}}'s reign in 697 until the 10th year of [[Emperor Kanmu]]'s reign in 791, spanning nine imperial reigns. It was completed in 797 AD.<ref>{{citation |url= http://journal.oraltradition.org/issues/24i/bender |title=Performative Loci of ''Shoku Nihongi'' Edicts, 749–770 |last= Bender|first= Ross|date= March 2009 |work= Journal of Oral Tradition|volume=24|issue=1|pages=249–268}}</ref>
The text is forty volumes in length. It is primarily written in {{ill|Kanbun|en|Kanbun|WD=Q7901319|lt=Kanbun}}, a Japanese form of {{ill|Classical Chinese|en|Classical Chinese|WD=Q37041|lt=Classical Chinese}}, as was normal for formal Japanese texts at the time.<ref>''Rikkokushi'' (『六国史』), by [[Tarō Sakamoto]] (坂本太郎) (Yoshikawa Kobunkan (吉川弘文館), 1970, republished 1994)</ref> However, a number of "senmyō" 宣命 or "imperial edicts" contained within the text are written in a script known as "senmyō-gaki", which preserves particles and verb endings phonographically.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1245 |title=Senmyō |access-date=2012-02-27 |last=Motosawa |first=Masafumi |date=2007-03-28 |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto }}</ref>

It was completed in 797 AD.<ref>{{citation |url= http://journal.oraltradition.org/issues/24i/bender |title=Performative Loci of ''Shoku Nihongi'' Edicts, 749–770 |last= Bender|first= Ross|date= March 2009 |work= Journal of Oral Tradition|volume=24|issue=1|pages=249–268}}</ref>

It is primarily written in {{ill|Kanbun|en|Kanbun|WD=Q7901319|lt=Kanbun}}, a Japanese form of {{ill|Classical Chinese|en|Classical Chinese|WD=Q37041|lt=Classical Chinese}}, as was normal for formal Japanese texts at the time.<ref>''Rikkokushi'' (『六国史』), by [[Tarō Sakamoto]] (坂本太郎) (Yoshikawa Kobunkan (吉川弘文館), 1970, republished 1994)</ref> However, a number of "senmyō" 宣命 or "imperial edicts" contained within the text are written in a script known as "senmyō-gaki", which preserves particles and verb endings phonographically.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1245 |title=Senmyō |access-date=2012-02-27 |last=Motosawa |first=Masafumi |date=2007-03-28 |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto }}</ref>

Completed in 797, it is the second of the ''[[Rikkokushi|Six National Histories]]'', coming directly after the {{lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} and followed by ''{{ill|Nihon Kōki|en|Nihon Kōki|WD=Q1077029|lt=Nihon Kōki}}''. [[:en:Fujiwara no Tsugutada|Fujiwara no Tsugutada]] and {{ill|Sugano no Mamichi|en|Sugano no Mamichi|WD=Q11619464|lt=Sugano no Mamichi}} served as the primary editors. It is one of the most important primary historical sources for information about Japan's {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}.
The work covers the 95-year period from the beginning of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en|Emperor Monmu|WD=Q357246|lt=Emperor Monmu}}'s reign in 697 until the 10th year of [[Emperor Kanmu]]'s reign in 791, spanning nine imperial reigns. It was completed in 797 AD.<ref>{{citation |url= http://journal.oraltradition.org/issues/24i/bender |title=Performative Loci of ''Shoku Nihongi'' Edicts, 749–770 |last= Bender|first= Ross|date= March 2009 |work= Journal of Oral Tradition|volume=24|issue=1|pages=249–268}}</ref>
The text is forty volumes in length. It is primarily written in {{ill|Kanbun|en|Kanbun|WD=Q7901319|lt=Kanbun}}, a Japanese form of {{ill|Classical Chinese|en|Classical Chinese|WD=Q37041|lt=Classical Chinese}}, as was normal for formal Japanese texts at the time.<ref>''Rikkokushi'' (『六国史』), by [[Tarō Sakamoto]] (坂本太郎) (Yoshikawa Kobunkan (吉川弘文館), 1970, republished 1994)</ref> However, a number of "senmyō" 宣命 or "imperial edicts" contained within the text are written in a script known as "senmyō-gaki", which preserves particles and verb endings phonographically.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1245 |title=Senmyō |access-date=2012-02-27 |last=Motosawa |first=Masafumi |date=2007-03-28 |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto }}</ref>

{{Short description|Ancient Japanese history text}}{{italic title}} {{Shintoism}} The {{nihongo|'''''Shoku Nihongi'''''|続日本紀|}} is an imperially-commissioned [[:en:Japanese history|Japanese history]] text. Completed in 797, it is the second of the ''[[Rikkokushi|Six National Histories]]'', coming directly after the {{lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} and followed by ''{{ill|Nihon Kōki|en|Nihon Kōki|WD=Q1077029|lt=Nihon Kōki}}''. [[:en:Fujiwara no Tsugutada|Fujiwara no Tsugutada]] and {{ill|Sugano no Mamichi|en|Sugano no Mamichi|WD=Q11619464|lt=Sugano no Mamichi}} served as the primary editors. It is one of the most important primary historical sources for information about Japan's {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}.
The work covers the 95-year period from the beginning of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en|Emperor Monmu|WD=Q357246|lt=Emperor Monmu}}'s reign in 697 until the 10th year of [[Emperor Kanmu]]'s reign in 791, spanning nine imperial reigns. It was completed in 797 AD.<ref>{{citation |url= http://journal.oraltradition.org/issues/24i/bender |title=Performative Loci of ''Shoku Nihongi'' Edicts, 749–770 |last= Bender|first= Ross|date= March 2009 |work= Journal of Oral Tradition|volume=24|issue=1|pages=249–268}}</ref>
The text is forty volumes in length. It is primarily written in {{ill|Kanbun|en|Kanbun|WD=Q7901319|lt=Kanbun}}, a Japanese form of {{ill|Classical Chinese|en|Classical Chinese|WD=Q37041|lt=Classical Chinese}}, as was normal for formal Japanese texts at the time.<ref>''Rikkokushi'' (『六国史』), by [[Tarō Sakamoto]] (坂本太郎) (Yoshikawa Kobunkan (吉川弘文館), 1970, republished 1994)</ref> However, a number of "senmyō" 宣命 or "imperial edicts" contained within the text are written in a script known as "senmyō-gaki", which preserves particles and verb endings phonographically.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1245 |title=Senmyō |access-date=2012-02-27 |last=Motosawa |first=Masafumi |date=2007-03-28 |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto }}</ref>
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== * {{NKBJ}} * {{NKBD}} *[http://www.j-texts.com/sheet/shoku.html Text of the ''Shoku Nihongi''] (Japanese) *[https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Shoku%20Nihongi.html Further information and text of the ''Shoku Nihongi''] at {{ill|Japanese Historical Text Initiative|en|Japanese Historical Text Initiative|WD=Q16973361|lt=Japanese Historical Text Initiative}} *[http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_02450/ri05_02450_0016/ manuscript scans at Waseda University Library]

It is primarily written in {{ill|Kanbun|en|Kanbun|WD=Q7901319|lt=Kanbun|1=Kanbun}}, a Japanese form of {{ill|Classical Chinese|en|Classical Chinese|WD=Q37041|lt=Classical Chinese|1=Classical Chinese}}, as was normal for formal Japanese texts at the time.<ref>''Rikkokushi'' (『六国史』), by [[Tarō Sakamoto]] (坂本太郎) (Yoshikawa Kobunkan (吉川弘文館), 1970, republished 1994)</ref> However, a number of "senmyō" 宣命 or "imperial edicts" contained within the text are written in a script known as "senmyō-gaki", which preserves particles and verb endings phonographically.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1245 |title=Senmyō |access-date=2012-02-27 |last=Motosawa |first=Masafumi |date=2007-03-28 |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto }}</ref>

{{Short description|Ancient Japanese history text}}{{italic title}} {{Shintoism}} The {{nihongo|'''''Shoku Nihongi'''''|続日本紀|}} is an imperially-commissioned [[:en:Japanese history|Japanese history]] text. Completed in 797, it is the second of the ''[[Rikkokushi|Six National Histories]]'', coming directly after the {{lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} and followed by ''{{ill|Nihon Kōki|en|Nihon Kōki|WD=Q1077029|lt=Nihon Kōki|1=Nihon Kōki}}''. [[:en:Fujiwara no Tsugutada|Fujiwara no Tsugutada]] and {{ill|Sugano no Mamichi|en|Sugano no Mamichi|WD=Q11619464|lt=Sugano no Mamichi|1=Sugano no Mamichi}} served as the primary editors. It is one of the most important primary historical sources for information about Japan's {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}.
The work covers the 95-year period from the beginning of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en|Emperor Monmu|WD=Q357246|lt=Emperor Monmu|1=Emperor Monmu}}'s reign in 697 until the 10th year of [[Emperor Kanmu]]'s reign in 791, spanning nine imperial reigns. It was completed in 797 AD.<ref>{{citation |url= http://journal.oraltradition.org/issues/24i/bender |title=Performative Loci of ''Shoku Nihongi'' Edicts, 749–770 |last= Bender|first= Ross|date= March 2009 |work= Journal of Oral Tradition|volume=24|issue=1|pages=249–268}}</ref>
The text is forty volumes in length. It is primarily written in {{ill|Kanbun|en|Kanbun|WD=Q7901319|lt=Kanbun|1=Kanbun}}, a Japanese form of {{ill|Classical Chinese|en|Classical Chinese|WD=Q37041|lt=Classical Chinese|1=Classical Chinese}}, as was normal for formal Japanese texts at the time.<ref>''Rikkokushi'' (『六国史』), by [[Tarō Sakamoto]] (坂本太郎) (Yoshikawa Kobunkan (吉川弘文館), 1970, republished 1994)</ref> However, a number of "senmyō" 宣命 or "imperial edicts" contained within the text are written in a script known as "senmyō-gaki", which preserves particles and verb endings phonographically.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1245 |title=Senmyō |access-date=2012-02-27 |last=Motosawa |first=Masafumi |date=2007-03-28 |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto }}</ref>

Completed in 797, it is the second of the ''[[Rikkokushi|Six National Histories]]'', coming directly after the {{lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} and followed by ''{{ill|Nihon Kōki|en|Nihon Kōki|WD=Q1077029|lt=Nihon Kōki|1=Nihon Kōki}}''. [[:en:Fujiwara no Tsugutada|Fujiwara no Tsugutada]] and {{ill|Sugano no Mamichi|en|Sugano no Mamichi|WD=Q11619464|lt=Sugano no Mamichi|1=Sugano no Mamichi}} served as the primary editors. It is one of the most important primary historical sources for information about Japan's {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}.
The work covers the 95-year period from the beginning of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en|Emperor Monmu|WD=Q357246|lt=Emperor Monmu|1=Emperor Monmu}}'s reign in 697 until the 10th year of [[Emperor Kanmu]]'s reign in 791, spanning nine imperial reigns. It was completed in 797 AD.<ref>{{citation |url= http://journal.oraltradition.org/issues/24i/bender |title=Performative Loci of ''Shoku Nihongi'' Edicts, 749–770 |last= Bender|first= Ross|date= March 2009 |work= Journal of Oral Tradition|volume=24|issue=1|pages=249–268}}</ref>
The text is forty volumes in length. It is primarily written in {{ill|Kanbun|en|Kanbun|WD=Q7901319|lt=Kanbun|1=Kanbun}}, a Japanese form of {{ill|Classical Chinese|en|Classical Chinese|WD=Q37041|lt=Classical Chinese|1=Classical Chinese}}, as was normal for formal Japanese texts at the time.<ref>''Rikkokushi'' (『六国史』), by [[Tarō Sakamoto]] (坂本太郎) (Yoshikawa Kobunkan (吉川弘文館), 1970, republished 1994)</ref> However, a number of "senmyō" 宣命 or "imperial edicts" contained within the text are written in a script known as "senmyō-gaki", which preserves particles and verb endings phonographically.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |url= http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1245 |title=Senmyō |access-date=2012-02-27 |last=Motosawa |first=Masafumi |date=2007-03-28 |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto }}</ref>
